<TR BGCOLOR="#cceeff">
<TD VALIGN="top" ALIGN="right"> <P STYLE="margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASP of new system sales</FONT></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>27.4</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">24.8</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
<TR>
<TD VALIGN="top" ALIGN="right"> <P STYLE="margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASP of used system sales</FONT></P></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>6.9</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">7.6</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="font-size:1pt">
<TD HEIGHT="5"></TD>
<TD HEIGHT="5" COLSPAN="4"></TD>
<TD HEIGHT="5" COLSPAN="4"></TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="font-size:1px">
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net sales increased by EUR 513.8&nbsp;million, or 10.9 percent, to EUR 5,245.3&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 4,731.5&nbsp;million
in 2012, mainly driven by the increase in net service and field option sales of EUR 322.3&nbsp;million, or 34.7 percent to EUR 1,252.2&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 929.9&nbsp;million in 2012. The increase in net service and field option sales was
mainly caused by the expansion of Holistic Lithography through increased sales of integrated metrology and feedback loop technology and by OnPulse revenues as a result of the acquisition of Cymer. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The increase in net system sales of EUR 191.5&nbsp;million, or 5.0 percent, to EUR 3,993.1&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 3,801.6&nbsp;million in 2012 was mainly
caused by an increased ASP which more than offset the lower amount of systems sold. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The increase of the ASP of our systems sold was mainly explained by
the ASP of our new systems sold which increased to EUR 27.4&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 24.8&nbsp;million in 2012, which was the result of a shift in the mix of systems sold towards more high-end system types, NXT:1960Bi and NXT:1970Ci and
including one NXE:3300B. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Through 2013, all of the top 10 chipmakers worldwide, in terms of semiconductor capital expenditure, were our customers. In
2013, recognized sales to our largest customer accounted for EUR 2,058.6&nbsp;million, or 39.2 percent of our net sales. In 2012, recognized sales to our largest customer accounted for EUR 1,236.1&nbsp;million, or 26.1 percent of our net sales.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Gross profit increased by EUR 172.0&nbsp;million to EUR 2,177.2&nbsp;million or 41.5 percent of net sales in 2013 from EUR 2,005.2&nbsp;million or 42.4
percent of net sales in 2012. Higher gross profit was mainly driven by the increased net system sales. Gross profit as a percentage of net sales in 2013 decreased compared to 2012, mainly due to a 1.6 percent impact from non-cash purchase price
accounting adjustments related to the Cymer acquisition. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Other Income </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Other income consisted of contributions for R&amp;D programs under the NRE funding arrangements from Participating Customers in the CCIP and amounted to EUR 64.4&nbsp;million for 2013 (2012: nil). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Research and Development Costs </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">R&amp;D costs (net of
credits and excluding contributions under the NRE funding agreements from Participating Customers in the CCIP) increased by EUR 292.9&nbsp;million, or 49.7 percent, to EUR 882.0&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 589.1&nbsp;million in 2012. R&amp;D
spending mainly increased due the additional investments in EUV source development as a result of the acquisition of Cymer and further investments in our other strategic programs (Immersion and holistic lithography). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Selling, General and Administrative Costs </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">SG&amp;A costs
increased by EUR 52.4&nbsp;million, or 20.2 percent, to EUR 311.7&nbsp;million in 2013, from EUR 259.3&nbsp;million in 2012. The increase was mainly driven by the acquisition and subsequent integration of Cymer and incurred restructuring expenses.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Interest and Other, Net </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Interest and other, net
increased by EUR 18.2&nbsp;million to EUR 24.4&nbsp;million expense in 2013 from EUR 6.2&nbsp;million expense in 2012. This increase was mainly explained by the loss on the partial extinguishment of our EUR 600&nbsp;million 5.75 percent senior notes
due 2017 and a lower yield generated on our cash and cash equivalents and short -term investments. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Income Taxes </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The effective tax rate increased to 0.8 percent of income before income taxes in 2013 compared to 0.4 percent in 2012. In 2013, the effective tax rate was impacted
by the settlement of the favorable pre-existing relationships between Cymer and ASML. This settlement gain was not recognized for tax purposes and was, apart from the R&amp;D tax incentives, the major driver for the change in the effective tax rate
in 2013. In 2012, the effective tax rate was amongst others impacted by a one-time tax benefit resulting from successful conclusion of tax audits. The movement in the liability for unrecognized tax benefits in 2013 was considered to be limited
compared to 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Net Income </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net income in 2013
amounted to EUR 1,015.5&nbsp;million, or 19.4 percent of net sales, representing EUR 2.36 basic net income per ordinary share, compared with net income in 2012 of EUR 1,146.3&nbsp;million, or 24.2 percent of net sales, representing EUR 2.70 basic
net income per ordinary share. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Foreign Exchange Management </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors &#150; Fluctuations in Foreign Exchange Rates Could Harm Our Results of Operations&#148;, Item&nbsp;11 &#147;Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;,
Note 1 and Note 4 to our Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_18"></A>B. Liquidity and Capital Resources </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our cash and cash equivalents increased to EUR 2,419.5&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 from EUR 2,330.7&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 and our
short-term investments decreased to EUR 334.9&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 from EUR 679.9&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We
generated net cash from operating activities of EUR 1,025.2&nbsp;million, EUR 1,054.2&nbsp;million and EUR 703.5&nbsp;million in 2014, 2013 and 2012, respectively. Lower net cash provided by operating activities in 2014 compared to 2013 relates to
increased working capital, which is mainly due to a decrease in accounts payable. Higher net cash provided by operating activities in 2013 compared to 2012 related to increased sales levels and decreased our working capital, mainly due to an
increase in accounts payable. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We used EUR 16.2&nbsp;million for investing activities in 2014, EUR 368.4&nbsp;million in 2013 and EUR
1,119.8&nbsp;million in 2012. In 2014, our investing activities mainly related to the purchase of property, plant and equipment of EUR 358.3&nbsp;million, mainly for the construction of our EUV production facilities in Veldhoven, the Netherlands.
This was offset by the decrease of short-term investments, mainly in Dutch Treasury Certificates and deposits with the Dutch government, of EUR 345.0 million. In 2013, our investing activities mainly related to the cash consideration paid for the
acquisition of Cymer of EUR 443.7&nbsp;million and the purchase of property, plant and equipment of EUR 210.8&nbsp;million, mainly related to further expansion of our EUV production facilities. This was to a large extent offset by the decrease of
short-term investments, mainly in Dutch Treasury Certificates and deposits with the Dutch government, of EUR 290.2 million. In 2012, our investing activities primarily related to our short-term investments in Dutch Treasury Certificates and deposits
with the Dutch government of EUR 930.0&nbsp;million and purchases of property, plant and equipment of EUR 171.9 million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net cash used in financing
activities was EUR 928.4&nbsp;million in 2014, EUR 113.1&nbsp;million in 2013, and EUR 545.6&nbsp;million in 2012. In 2014, net cash used in financing activities included a cash outflow of EUR 700.0&nbsp;million for our regular share buyback program
and our annual dividend payment of EUR 268.0&nbsp;million, slightly offset by EUR 39.7&nbsp;million net proceeds from issuance of shares in connection with the exercise and purchase of employee stock and stock options. In 2013, net cash used in
financing activities included the net cash proceeds of EUR 740.4&nbsp;million from our notes offering and net cash proceeds of EUR 31.8&nbsp;million from the issuance of shares in connection with the exercise and purchase of employee stock options,
mainly offset by net cash outflows of EUR 368.3&nbsp;million for our notes tender offer and unwinding of related interest rate swaps, EUR 300.0&nbsp;million for our regular share buyback program and EUR 216.1&nbsp;million for our annual dividend
payment. In 2012, net cash used in financing activities included the net cash outflow of EUR 3,728.3&nbsp;million for the synthetic share buyback, EUR 535.4&nbsp;million for our regular share buyback programs and EUR 188.9&nbsp;million for our
annual dividend payment, to a large extent offset by the proceeds of EUR 3,853.9&nbsp;million from issuance of shares under the CCIP and EUR 53.8&nbsp;million net proceeds from issuance of shares in connection with the exercise and purchase of
employee stock options. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our principal sources of liquidity consist of cash flows from operations, cash and cash equivalents as of December&nbsp;31,
2014 of EUR 2,419.5&nbsp;million, short-term investments as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 of EUR 334.9&nbsp;million and available credit facilities as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 of EUR 700.0 million. In addition, we may from time to time raise additional
capital in debt and equity markets. Our goal is to remain an investment grade rated company and maintain a capital structure that supports this. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We invest our cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments in short-term deposits with financial institutions
that have good credit ratings and with the Dutch government, in Dutch Treasury Certificates and in AAAm-rated money market funds that invest in high-rated short-term debt securities of financial institutions and governments. Our investments are
denominated in euros. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our available credit facility consists of a EUR 700.0&nbsp;million committed revolving credit facility from a group of banks that
will mature in 2018. The credit facility contains a restrictive covenant that requires us to maintain a minimum committed capital to net total assets ratio of 40.0 percent calculated in accordance with contractually agreed definitions. At the end of
2014, we were in compliance with the covenant. Outstanding amounts under this credit facility will bear interest at EURIBOR or LIBOR plus a margin that depends on our liquidity position. No amounts were outstanding under the credit facility as of
December&nbsp;31, 2014. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We have repayment obligations in 2017, amounting to EUR 238.2&nbsp;million and in 2023, amounting to EUR 750.0&nbsp;million,
both relating to our Eurobonds. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML seeks to ensure that cash generated from operations, together with the liquidity provided by existing cash and
cash equivalents and short-term investments and its borrowing capability, will be sufficient to satisfy its liquidity requirements throughout every phase of the industry cycles. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our liquidity needs are affected by many factors, some of which are based on the normal on-going operations of the business, and others that relate to the uncertainties of the global economy and the semiconductor
industry. Although our cash requirements fluctuate based on the timing and extent of these factors, we believe that cash generated from operations, together with the liquidity provided by existing cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments
and our borrowing capability are sufficient to satisfy our current requirements, including our expected capital expenditures in 2015. We intend to return cash to our shareholders on a regular basis in the form of dividend payments and, subject to
our actual and anticipated liquidity requirements and other relevant factors, share buybacks or capital repayment. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Notes 4, 5, 15, 16, 26 and 27 to
our Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_19"></A>C. Research and Development, Patents and Licenses, etc. </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Research and Development </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Item&nbsp;4.B. &#147;Business
Overview &#150; Research and Development&#148; and Item&nbsp;5.A. &#147;Operating Results &#150; Results of Operations&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:14px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Intellectual Property
Matters </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors &#150; Failure to Adequately Protect the Intellectual Property Rights Upon Which We depend Could Harm
Our Business&#148; and &#147;Risk Factors &#150; Defending Against Intellectual Property Claims by Others Could Harm Our Business&#148; and Item&nbsp;4.B. &#147;Business Overview &#150; Intellectual Property&#148;. </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_20"></A>D. Trend Information </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In EUV, we have made significant progress and we have met the 500-wafer-per-day target that our customers set for us in 2014 as we have now demonstrated these productivity levels at multiple customers over multiple
days. We also demonstrated source power at 100 Watts with 96 percent die yield, a key factor to get to the productivity levels that our customers need for volume production. Our 2015 productivity target remains at 1,000 wafers per day and
importantly for our customers and for our EUV program, we received the first 2 orders for our fourth-generation NXE:3350B systems, the first of which is planned for shipment mid-year 2015. In addition, two NXE:3300B systems already delivered will be
upgraded to NXE:3350B performance. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Looking ahead to the first half of 2015 for DUV, we expect both our sales to the Memory segment and our service and
field option business to continue to be strong and sales to the logic segment to increase from the second half year of 2014 to the first half year of 2015. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The following table sets forth our systems backlog as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 and 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

<TR>
<TD WIDTH="2%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">1</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our systems backlog and net bookings include sales orders for which written authorizations have been accepted and shipment and/or revenue recognition is expected within 12
months. As of 2014 we also include EUV in our backlog starting with our NXE:3350B systems. As a result, two NXE:3350B systems are now included in our systems backlog and net bookings. Before 2014, our systems backlog and net bookings include only
sales orders for which written authorizations have been accepted and system shipment and revenue recognition dates within the following 12 months have been assigned. This change has no impact on the comparative figures. </FONT></TD></TR></TABLE>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Historically, orders have been subject to cancellation or delay by the customer. Due to possible customer changes in delivery schedules and to cancellation of
orders, our systems backlog at any particular date is not necessarily indicative of actual sales for any succeeding period. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For the first quarter of
2015, we expect net sales of around EUR 1.6 billion, a gross margin of around 47 percent, R&amp;D costs of about EUR 260&nbsp;million, other income of about EUR 20&nbsp;million, which consists of contributions from participants of the CCIP and
SG&amp;A costs of about EUR 83 million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">On January&nbsp;21, 2015 we announced a new share buyback program, to be executed within the 2015-2016
timeframe. As part of this program, we intend to purchase up to EUR 750&nbsp;million of shares which we intend to cancel upon repurchase. In addition, we intend to purchase as part of this program up to 3.3&nbsp;million shares to cover employee
stock and stock option plans. This buyback program will start on January&nbsp;22, 2015, and at current share price these intended repurchases represent a total value of approximately EUR 1 billion. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The trends discussed in this Item&nbsp;5.D. &#147;Trend information&#148; are subject to risks and uncertainties. See &#147;Part I &#150; Special Note Regarding
Forward Looking Statements&#148; and item 3.D. &#147;Risk Factors&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_21"></A>E. Off-Balance Sheet Arrangements
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We have various contractual obligations, some of which are required to be recognized as liabilities in our Financial Statements, including long- and
short-term debt. Other contractual obligations, namely operating lease commitments, purchase obligations and guarantees, are generally not required to be recognized as liabilities on our balance sheet but are required to be disclosed. </FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_22"></A>F. Tabular Disclosure of Contractual Obligations </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our contractual obligations as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 can be summarized as follows: </FONT></P>

<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Note 15 to our Financial Statements for the amounts excluding interest expense. </FONT></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Long-term debt obligations mainly relate to interest payments and principal amounts of our Eurobonds. See Note 15 to our Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Operating lease obligations include leases of equipment and facilities. Lease payments recognized as an expense were EUR 43.9&nbsp;million, EUR 42.0&nbsp;million and EUR 41.6&nbsp;million for the years ended
December&nbsp;31, 2014, 2013 and 2012, respectively. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Several operating leases for our buildings contain purchase options, exercisable at the end of the
lease, and in some cases, during the term of the lease. The amounts to be paid if ASML should exercise these purchase options at the end of the lease as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 can be summarized as follows: </FONT></P>

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Purchase obligations include purchase commitments with suppliers in the ordinary course of business. ASML expects that it will
honor these purchase obligations to fulfill future sales, in line with the timing of those future sales. The general terms and conditions of the agreements relating to the major part of our purchase commitments as of December&nbsp;31, 2014 contain
clauses that enable us to delay or cancel delivery of ordered goods and services up to the dates specified in the corresponding purchase contracts. These terms and conditions that we typically agree with our supply chain partners gives us additional
flexibility to adapt our purchase obligations to our requirements in light of the inherent cyclicality of the industry in which we operate. We establish a provision for cancellation costs when it is probable that the liability has been incurred and
the amount of cancellation fees is reasonably estimable. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_23"></A>G. Safe Harbor </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Part I &#147;Special Note Regarding Forward-Looking Statements&#148;. </FONT></P>

<TD WIDTH="2%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">5</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich is to retire by rotation at the 2015 AGM. See below discussion of nominees for appointment to the Supervisory Board at the 2015 AGM.
</FONT></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Bilous retired from the Supervisory Board per the 2014 AGM. Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich retired by rotation and was reappointed for a
period of one year in line with the Supervisory Board&#146;s rotation schedule. At the 2014 AGM Mr.&nbsp;Stork was appointed as member of the Supervisory Board for a period of four years. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">With great sadness, the Supervisory Board remembers the late Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg, member of our Supervisory Board and member of our Selection and Nomination Committee and Remuneration Committee, who passed away
on September&nbsp;28, 2014. Over the past nine years, Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg was an engaged and knowledgeable member of our Supervisory Board. The Supervisory Board is grateful for the time she dedicated to ASML. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Works Council has an enhanced right to make recommendations for nomination of one-third of the members of the Supervisory Board, which recommendations may be
rejected by the Supervisory Board in limited circumstances. See Item&nbsp;6.C. &#147;Board Practices &#151; Supervisory Board&#148;. At the Annual General Meeting of Shareholders held in 2009, Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr was appointed pursuant to
this recommendation right, and at the 2013 AGM she was reappointed in accordance with this recommendation right. At the 2014 AGM, Mr.&nbsp;Stork was appointed pursuant to this recommendation right. The appointment of Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg&#146;s
successor at the 2015 AGM will be based on this enhanced recommendation right. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Supervisory Board spent considerable time discussing its future
composition, in view of the rotation schedule and envisaged changes in 2015 and subsequent years. For the fulfillment of vacancies several factors are taken into consideration. The Supervisory Board profile includes the intention to have at least 30
percent representation of each gender in ASML&#146;s Supervisory Board. This aspect has been taken into account in the process that has led to the nomination of the following three new Supervisory Board members to the 2015 AGM: Ms.&nbsp;A. (Annet)
Aris, Mr.&nbsp;G.J. (Gerard) Kleisterlee and Mr.&nbsp;R.D. (Rolf-Dieter) Schwalb. The agenda and explanatory notes for the 2015 AGM will contain further information in this respect. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Supervisory Board reappointed Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury for another period of four years upon notification to the 2014 AGM. The indefinite appointment term of Mr.&nbsp;Wennink and Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink was
converted into a definite appointment term of four years per the 2014 AGM. Mr.&nbsp;Nickl was appointed as member of the BoM upon notification to the 2014 AGM. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">There are no family relationships among the members of our Supervisory Board and our BoM. </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Director and Officer Biographies </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Arthur P.M. van der Poel </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel was appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2004 and
was appointed as Chairman in March 2007. Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel was CEO of Philips Semiconductors, a member of the Board of Management and a member of the Group Management Committee of Royal Philips Electronics N.V. Currently, Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel
is a member of the Board of Directors of Gemalto Holding N.V. and the Chairman of the Supervisory Board of BDR Thermea. Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel was a member of the Supervisory Board of Royal HaskoningDHV B.V. until April&nbsp;1, 2014. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Fritz W. Fr&ouml;hlich </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich was
appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2004. Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich was Deputy Chairman and CFO of Akzo Nobel N.V. Currently, Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich is the Chairman of the Supervisory Board of Randstad Holding N.V., a member of the Supervisory
Boards of Allianz Nederland N.V. and Rexel S.A. and a member of the Board of Directors of Prysmian Group. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Pauline F.M. van der Meer Mohr
</I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr was appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2009. Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr was managing partner of the
Amstelbridge Group, Senior Executive Vice President at ABN AMRO Bank, Head of Group Human Resources at TNT N.V., and has held several senior executive roles at the Royal/Dutch Shell group of companies in various areas. Currently, Ms.&nbsp;Van der
Meer Mohr serves as President of the Executive Board of the Erasmus University Rotterdam, the Netherlands. Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr is also a member of the Supervisory Board of Royal DSM N.V., the Chairperson of the Executive Board of the
Fulbright Center and a member of the Board Concertgebouw Fonds. Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr was the Chairperson of the Supervisory Board of Rotterdam School of Management until December&nbsp;1, 2014. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Wolfgang H. Ziebart </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart was appointed
to our Supervisory Board in March 2009. Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart was President and CEO of Infineon Technologies A.G. Prior to that, Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart was on the Boards of Management of car components manufacturer Continental A.G. and automobile producer BMW
A.G. Currently, Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart is the Group Engineering Director of Jaguar Land Rover Ltd. and is the Chairman of the Supervisory Board of Nordex S.E. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Clara (Carla) M.S. Smits-Nusteling </I></B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling was appointed to our Supervisory Board in April 2013. Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling was CFO and a member of the Board of Management of Royal KPN
N.V. Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling also held several finance and business related positions at Royal KPN N.V. and PostNL. Currently, Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling is a non-executive director of the Board of Tele2 AB. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Douglas A. Grose </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Grose was appointed to our
Supervisory Board in April 2013. Mr.&nbsp;Grose was CEO of GlobalFoundries. Mr.&nbsp;Grose also served as senior vice president of technology development, manufacturing and supply chain for AMD. Prior to that, Mr.&nbsp;Grose spent 25 years at IBM as
General Manager of technology development and manufacturing for the systems and technology group. Currently, Mr.&nbsp;Grose is a member of the Board of Directors of SBA Materials. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Johannes (Hans) M.C. Stork </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Stork was appointed to our Supervisory Board in April 2014.
Mr.&nbsp;Stork held various management positions at IBM Corporation, Hewlett Packard Company, Texas Instruments, Inc. and Applied Materials, Inc., including Senior Vice President and CTO of Texas Instruments, Inc. and Group Vice President and CTO of
Applied Materials, Inc. Further, Mr.&nbsp;Stork was a member of the Board of Sematech. Currently, Mr.&nbsp;Stork serves as Senior Vice President and CTO of ON Semiconductor Corporation and is also a member of the Scientific Advisory Board of imec.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Peter T.F.M. Wennink </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Wennink
joined ASML on January&nbsp;1, 1999 and was appointed as Executive Vice President, CFO and member of our BoM on July&nbsp;1, 1999. Mr.&nbsp;Wennink was appointed as President and CEO on July&nbsp;1, 2013. Mr.&nbsp;Wennink has an extensive background
in finance and accounting. Prior to his employment with ASML, Mr.&nbsp;Wennink worked as a partner at Deloitte Accountants, specializing in the high technology industry with an emphasis on the semiconductor equipment industry. Mr.&nbsp;Wennink is a
member of the Dutch Institute of Registered Accountants, a member of the Supervisory Board of Bank Insinger de Beaufort N.V. and a member of the Advisory Board of the Investment Committee of Stichting Pensioenfonds ABP (Dutch pension fund for
government employees). Mr.&nbsp;Wennink further serves on the board of the FME-CWM (the employers&#146; organization for the technology industry in the Netherlands). </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Martin A. van den Brink </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink joined ASML when the company was founded in 1984. Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink held several positions in engineering and from 1995 he served as Vice President Technology. Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink was
appointed as Executive Vice President Product&nbsp;&amp; Technology and member of the BoM in 1999. On July&nbsp;1, 2013, Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink was appointed as President and CTO. Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink earned a degree in Electrical Engineering from
HTS Arnhem (HAN University), and a degree in Physics (1984)&nbsp;from the University of Twente, the Netherlands. In 2012, he was awarded an honorary doctorate in physics by the UvA, the Netherlands. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Frits J. van Hout </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout joined ASML in
1984 and rejoined ASML in 2001, after a nine years absence. He was appointed as a member of our BoM on March&nbsp;26, 2009. Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout was appointed as Executive Vice President and CPO on July&nbsp;1, 2013. Prior to that, Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout
served as Executive Vice President and CMO, Executive Vice President Integral Efficiency, Senior Vice President Customer Support and held various other positions. From 1992 until 2001, Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout served as CEO of the Beyeler Group and held
various management positions at Datacolor International. Mr.&nbsp;van Hout earned a Master&#146;s degree in Theoretical Physics (1981), University of Oxford; and a Master&#146;s degree in Applied Physics (1984), Eidgen&ouml;ssische Technische
Hochschule, Z&uuml;rich. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Fr&eacute;d&eacute;ric J.M. Schneider-Maunoury </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury joined ASML on December&nbsp;1, 2009, as Executive Vice President and COO and was appointed to our BoM on March&nbsp;24, 2010. Prior to joining ASML, Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury served
as Vice President Thermal Products Manufacturing of the power generation and rail transport equipment group ALSTOM. Previously, Mr. Schneider-Maunoury was general manager of the worldwide Hydro Business of ALSTOM. Further,
Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury held various positions at the French Ministry of Trade and Industry. Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury is a graduate of Ecole Polytechnique (1985)&nbsp;and Ecole Nationale Sup&eacute;rieure des Mines (1988)&nbsp;in Paris.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Wolfgang U. Nickl </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Nickl joined
ASML on December&nbsp;1, 2013, as Executive Vice President and CFO and was appointed as a member of our BoM per the 2014 AGM. Prior to joining ASML, Mr.&nbsp;Nickl served as Executive Vice President and CFO at Western Digital Corporation, a
U.S.-headquartered, NASDAQ-listed developer and manufacturer of storage devices, where he held several financial and operational leadership roles. Before Western Digital, Mr.&nbsp;Nickl gained experience in finance and IT consulting. He earned a BA
in Business from the University of Cooperative Education in Stuttgart, Germany, and an MBA from the University of Southern California&#146;s Marshall School of Business in Los Angeles, United States. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_26"></A>B. Compensation </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The information
required by Item&nbsp;6.B. is incorporated by reference from our 2014 Remuneration Report (pages 14,15,16, 18 and 19) which is included as exhibit 99.2 on Form 6-K furnished with the SEC on February&nbsp;11, 2015. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_27"></A>C. Board Practices </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>General
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We endorse the importance of good corporate governance, of which independent supervision, accountability and transparency are the most significant
elements. Within the framework of corporate governance, it is important that a relationship of trust exists between the BoM, the Supervisory Board, our employees and our shareholders. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We pursue a policy of active communication with our shareholders. In addition to the exchange of ideas at the General Meeting of Shareholders, other important forms of communication are the publication of our
annual and quarterly financial results as well as press releases and publications posted on our Website. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our corporate governance structure is intended
to: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Provide shareholders with regular, reliable, relevant and transparent information regarding our activities, structure, financial condition and results of
operations, performance and other information, including information on our social, ethical and environmental matters and policies; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Apply high-quality standards for disclosure, accounting and auditing; and </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Apply stringent rules with regard to insider securities trading. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Two-Tier Board Structure </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML is incorporated under Dutch law and has a two-tier board structure. Responsibility for the management of ASML lies with the BoM. Independent, non-executive members serve on the Supervisory Board, which
supervises and advises the members of the BoM in performing their management tasks. The BoM has the duty to keep the Supervisory Board informed, consult with the Supervisory Board on important matters and submit certain important decisions to the
Supervisory Board for its approval. The Supervisory Board is responsible for supervising, monitoring and advising the BoM on: (i)&nbsp;the achievement of our objectives, (ii)&nbsp;our strategy and management of risks inherent to our business
activities, (iii)&nbsp;the structure and operation of internal risk management and control systems, (iv)&nbsp;the financial reporting process and (v)&nbsp;compliance with applicable legislation and regulations. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Supervisory Board members are prohibited from serving as officers or employees of ASML, and members of the BoM cannot serve on the Supervisory Board. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Board of Management </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The BoM consists of at least two
members or such larger number of members as determined by the Supervisory Board. Members of the BoM are appointed by the Supervisory Board. The Supervisory Board must notify the General Meeting of Shareholders of the intended appointment of a member
of the BoM. As a result of our compliance with the Dutch Corporate Governance Code, members of the BoM shall be appointed for a maximum period of four years, but may be re-appointed. Members of the BoM serve until the end of the term of their
appointment, voluntary retirement, or suspension or dismissal by the Supervisory Board. In the case of dismissal, the Supervisory Board must first inform the General Meeting of Shareholders of the intended removal. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Supervisory Board determines the remuneration of the individual members of the BoM, in line with the remuneration policy adopted by the General Meeting of
Shareholders, upon a proposal of the Supervisory Board. ASML&#146;s remuneration policy is included in the 2014 Remuneration Report. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For more details
on the BoM, see Item&nbsp;6.A &#147;Directors and Senior Management&#148; and Item&nbsp;6.B. &#147;Compensation&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Supervisory Board
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Supervisory Board consists of at least three members or such larger number as determined by the Supervisory Board. The Supervisory Board
prepares a profile in relation to its size and composition; ASML&#146;s Supervisory Board profile is posted on ASML&#146;s Website. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Members of the
Supervisory Board are appointed by the General Meeting of Shareholders from nominations of the Supervisory Board. Nominations must be reasoned and must be made available to the General Meeting of Shareholders and the Works Council simultaneously.
Before the Supervisory Board presents its nominations, both the General Meeting of Shareholders and the Works Council may make recommendations (which the Supervisory Board may reject). In addition, the Works Council has an enhanced right to make
recommendations for nomination of at least one-third of the members of the Supervisory Board, which recommendation may only be rejected by the Supervisory Board: (i)&nbsp;if the relevant person is unsuitable or (ii)&nbsp;if the Supervisory Board
would not be duly composed if the recommended person were appointed as a Supervisory Board member. If no agreement can be reached between the Supervisory Board and the Works Council on these recommendations, the Supervisory Board may request the
Enterprise Chamber of the Amsterdam Court of Appeal to declare its objection legitimate. Any decision of the Enterprise Chamber on this matter is non-appealable. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Nominations of the Supervisory Board may be rejected by the General Meeting of Shareholders by an absolute majority of the votes representing at least one-third of the total outstanding capital. If the votes cast
in favor of such resolution do not represent at least one-third of the total outstanding capital, a new meeting can be convened at which the nomination can be rejected by an absolute majority. If a nomination is rejected, the Supervisory Board must
make a new nomination. If a nomination is not rejected and the General Meeting of Shareholders does not appoint the nominated person, the Supervisory Board will appoint the nominated person. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Members of the Supervisory Board serve for a maximum term of four years from the date of their appointment, or a shorter period as set out in the rotation schedule as adopted by the Supervisory Board. They may be
re-appointed, provided that their entire term of office does not exceed twelve years. The General Meeting of Shareholders may, with an absolute majority of the votes representing at least one-third of the total outstanding capital, dismiss the
Supervisory Board in its entirety for lack of confidence. In such event, the Enterprise Chamber of the Amsterdam Court of Appeal shall appoint new members of the Supervisory Board at the request of the BoM. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Upon the proposal of the Supervisory Board, the General Meeting of Shareholders determines the remuneration of the members of the Supervisory Board. A member of
the Supervisory Board may not be granted any shares or option rights by way of remuneration. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For more details on the Supervisory Board, see Item&nbsp;6.A &#147;Directors and Senior Management&#148; and
Item&nbsp;6.B.&#147;Compensation&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Approval of Board of Management Decisions </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The BoM requires prior approval of the General Meeting of Shareholders for resolutions concerning an important change in the identity or character of ASML or its business, including: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">A transfer of all or substantially all of the business of ASML to a third party; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Entering into or the termination of a long-term material joint venture between ASML and a third party; and </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">An acquisition or divestment by ASML of an interest in the capital of a company with a value of at least one-third of ASML&#146;s assets (determined by reference
to ASML&#146;s most recently adopted Statutory Annual Report). </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Rules of Procedure </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The BoM and the Supervisory Board have adopted Rules of Procedure for each of the BoM, Supervisory Board and the four Committees of the Supervisory Board. These
Rules of Procedure are posted on our Website. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Directors and Officers Insurance and Indemnification </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Members of the BoM and Supervisory Board, as well as certain senior management members, are insured under ASML&#146;s Directors and Officers Insurance Policy.
Although the insurance policy provides for broad coverage, our directors and officers may incur uninsured liabilities. ASML has agreed to indemnify its members of the BoM and Supervisory Board against any claims arising in connection with their
position within ASML, provided that such claim is not attributable to willful misconduct or intentional recklessness. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Corporate Governance
Developments </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML continuously monitors and assesses applicable corporate governance rules, including recommendations and initiatives regarding
principles of corporate governance. These include rules that have been promulgated in the United States both by NASDAQ and by the SEC. See also Item&nbsp;16.G. &#147;Corporate Governance&#148;. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Dutch Corporate Governance Code came into effect on January&nbsp;1, 2004 and was amended as of January&nbsp;1, 2009. Dutch listed companies are required to
either comply with the principles and the best practice provisions of the Code, or to explain on which points they deviate from these best practice provisions and why. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML reports on its compliance with the Dutch Corporate Governance Code in its 2014 Statutory Annual Report. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Committees of ASML&#146;s Supervisory Board </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">While
retaining overall responsibility, the Supervisory Board assigns certain of its tasks to its four Committees: the Audit Committee, the Remuneration Committee, the Selection and Nomination Committee and the Technology and Strategy Committee. Members
of these Committees are appointed from among the Supervisory Board members. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Chairman of each Committee reports to the Supervisory Board the issues
and items discussed in each meeting. In addition, the minutes of each Committee are available to all members of the Supervisory Board, enabling the Supervisory Board to make the appropriate decisions. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Audit Committee </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The current members of our Audit
Committee are Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich (Chairman), Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel, Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling and Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr, each of whom is an independent, non-executive member of our Supervisory Board in accordance with the NASDAQ Listing
Rules. The Supervisory Board has determined that Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich qualifies as the Audit Committee financial expert pursuant to Section&nbsp;407 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act and the rules promulgated thereunder. Our external auditor, CEO, CFO,
Corporate Controller, Corporate Chief Accountant, Senior Director Corporate Risk and Assurance, as well as other ASML employees invited by the Chairman of the Audit Committee may also attend the meetings of the Audit Committee. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Audit Committee assists the Supervisory Board in: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Overseeing the integrity of our Financial Statements and related financial and non-financial disclosures; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>

<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Overseeing the qualifications, independence and performance of the external auditor; and </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>

<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Overseeing our disclosure controls and procedures (as defined in the Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e)) and internal control over financial reporting
(as defined in Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(f) and 15d-15(f)). </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In 2014, the Audit Committee held eight scheduled meetings, either in
person or via conference call. </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Remuneration Committee </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The current members of our Remuneration Committee are Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart (Chairman) and Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr, each of whom is an independent, non-executive member of our Supervisory Board in accordance with the
NASDAQ Listing Rules. In 2014, the Remuneration Committee held four scheduled meetings and several ad hoc meetings, either in person or via conference call. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For details on the Remuneration Committee See Item&nbsp;6.B. &#147;Compensation&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Selection and
Nomination Committee </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The current members of our Selection and Nomination Committee are Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel (Chairman), Mr.&nbsp;Grose and
Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich, each of whom is an independent, non-executive member of our Supervisory Board in accordance with the NASDAQ Listing Rules. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The
Selection and Nomination Committee assists the Supervisory Board in: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">

<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Periodically evaluating the scope and composition of the BoM, the Supervisory Board, and proposing the profile of the Supervisory Board in relation thereto;

Supervisory Board; and </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">

criteria for senior management. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Selection and Nomination Committee furthermore discusses imminent corporate governance
developments, for example those based on legislative proposals, but also the outcome of the Report of the Monitoring Committee with respect to compliance with the Code. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In 2014, the Selection and Nomination Committee held three scheduled meetings and several ad hoc meetings, either in person or by conference call. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Technology and Strategy Committee </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The current members of our Technology and Strategy Committee are
Mr.&nbsp;Grose (Chairman), Mr.&nbsp;Stork, Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel and Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart. The Technology and Strategy Committee may appoint one or more advisors from within and/or from outside ASML. The advisors to the Technology and Strategy Committee
may be invited as guests to the meetings, or parts thereof, but are not entitled to vote in the meetings. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Technology and Strategy Committee assists
the Supervisory Board in relation to the following responsibilities and may prepare resolutions for the Supervisory Board related thereto: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Familiarization with and risk assessment and study of potential strategies, required technical resources, technology roadmaps and product roadmaps; and
</FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Providing advice to the Supervisory Board with respect to matters related thereto. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In 2014, the Technology and Strategy Committee held six scheduled meetings, either in person or via conference call. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Disclosure Committee </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML has a Disclosure
Committee to ensure compliance with applicable disclosure requirements arising under US and Dutch law and applicable stock exchange rules, US&nbsp;GAAP, IFRS as adopted by the EU and the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. The Disclosure Committee is composed of
various members of senior management, and reports to the CEO and CFO. The Disclosure Committee informs the Audit Committee about the outcome of the Disclosure Committee meetings. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Disclosure Committee gathers all relevant financial and non-financial information and assesses materiality, timeliness and necessity for disclosure of such information. In addition the Disclosure Committee
advises the CEO and CFO on the effectiveness of the disclosure controls and procedures and of the internal control over financial reporting (Sarbanes-Oxley Act). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">During 2014, the Disclosure Committee reviewed amongst others the quarterly and annual financial result announcements, the Statutory Interim Report, the Annual Report on Form 20-F and the Statutory Annual Report.
In addition, the Disclosure Committee also advised the CEO and CFO on the effectiveness of the disclosure controls and procedures and of the internal control over financial reporting (Sarbanes-Oxley Act). </FONT></P>

STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">During 2014, the average number of payroll employees in FTEs employed was 10,942, and the average number of temporary employees in FTEs employed was 2,804. The increase in 2014 compared to 2013 payroll employees in
FTEs is in line with our net sales growth. The increase in employees expressed in FTEs in 2013 compared to 2012 was mainly due to the acquisition of Cymer as per May&nbsp;30, 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For a more detailed description of our payroll employee information, see Notes 18 and 22 to our Financial Statements. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our future success also depends on our ability to attract, train, retain and motivate highly qualified, skilled and educated employees, who are in great demand. We
are particularly reliant on the services of several key employees, including a number of systems development specialists with advanced university qualifications in engineering, optics and computing. See Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors &#150; Our
Business and Future Success Depend on Our Ability to Attract and Retain a Sufficient Number of Adequately Educated and Skilled Employees.&#148; </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML
Netherlands B.V., our operating subsidiary in the Netherlands, has a Works Council, as required by Dutch law. A Works Council is a representative body of the employees of a Dutch company elected by the employees. The Board of Management of any Dutch
company that runs an enterprise with a Works Council must seek the non-binding advice of the Works Council before taking certain decisions with respect to ASML, such as those related to a major restructuring, a change of control, or the appointment
or dismissal of a member of the Board of Management. In case the Works Council renders a contrary advice on a particular decision and the Board of Management nonetheless wishes to proceed, the Board of Management must temporarily suspend any further
action while the Works Council determines whether to appeal to the Enterprise Chamber of the Amsterdam Court of Appeal. Other decisions directly involving employment matters that apply either to all employees, or certain groups of employees, may
only be taken with the Works Council&#146;s approval. Failing approval of the Works Council, the decision first has to be submitted to the Enterprise Chamber of the Amsterdam Court of Appeal for mediation. If no resolution has been reached, the
decision can only be taken by with the approval of the Dutch District Court. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc737660_29"></A>E. Share Ownership </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For information with respect to the grant of shares and stock options to our employees See Note 18 to our Financial Statements, Item&nbsp;6.B.
&#147;Compensation&#148; and Item&nbsp;7A &#147;Major Shareholders&#148;. </FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Obligations of Shareholders to Disclose Holdings under Dutch Law </U></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Holders of our shares may be subject to reporting obligations under the FMSA. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The disclosure obligations under the FMSA apply to any person or entity that acquires, holds or disposes of an interest in the voting rights and/or the capital of a public limited company incorporated under the
laws of the Netherlands whose shares are admitted to trading on a regulated market within the European Union, such as ASML. Disclosure is required when the percentage of voting rights or capital interest of a person or an entity reaches, exceeds or
falls below 3.0, 5.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 25.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 60.0, 75.0 or 95.0 percent (as a result of an acquisition or disposal by such person, or as a result of a change in our total number of voting rights or capital issued). With respect to
ASML, the FMSA requires any person or entity whose interest in the voting rights and/or capital of ASML reached, exceeded or fell below those percentage interests to notify the AFM immediately. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML is required to notify the AFM immediately if our voting rights and/or capital have changed by 1.0 percent or more since its previous notification on
outstanding voting rights and capital. In addition, ASML must notify the AFM of changes of less than 1.0 percent in ASML&#146;s outstanding voting rights and capital at least once per calendar quarter, within eight days after the end of the quarter.
Any person whose direct or indirect voting rights and/or capital interest meets or passes the thresholds referred to in the previous paragraph as a result of a change in the outstanding voting rights or capital must notify the AFM no later than the
fourth trading day after the AFM has published such a change. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Once every calendar year, within four weeks after the end of the calendar year, holders
of an interest of 3.0 percent or more in ASML&#146;s voting rights or capital must notify the AFM of any changes in the composition of their interest resulting from certain acts (including, but not limited to, the exchange of shares for depositary
receipts and vice versa, and the exercise of rights to acquire shares). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Subsidiaries, as defined in the FMSA, do not have independent reporting
obligations under the FMSA, as interests held by them are attributed to their (ultimate) parents. Any person may qualify as a parent for purposes of the FMSA, including an individual. A person who ceases to be a subsidiary and who disposes of an
interest of 3.0 percent or more in ASML&#146;s voting rights or capital must immediately notify the AFM. As of that moment, all notification obligations under the FMSA become applicable to the former subsidiary. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For the purpose of calculating the percentage of capital interest or voting rights, the following interests must, among other arrangements, be taken into account:
shares and votes (i)&nbsp;directly held by any person, (ii)&nbsp;held by such person&#146;s subsidiaries, (iii)&nbsp;held by a third party for such person&#146;s account, (iv)&nbsp;held by a third party with whom such person has concluded an oral or
written voting agreement (including on the basis of an unrestricted power of attorney), (v)&nbsp;held by a third party with whom such person has agreed to temporarily transfer voting rights against payment, (vi)&nbsp;financial instruments of which
the increase in value is wholly or partially dependent on an increase in value of our shares or distributions in respect thereof (including certain cash settled financial instruments such as contracts for difference and total return swaps),
(vii)&nbsp;put options pursuant to which a person can be required to purchase our shares, and (vii)&nbsp;other contracts under which a person has a position economically comparable to having our shares. Interests held jointly by multiple persons are
attributed to those persons in accordance with their entitlement. A holder of a pledge or right of usufruct in respect of shares can also be subject to these reporting obligations if such person has, or can acquire, the right to vote on the shares
or, in case of depositary receipts, the underlying shares. The managers of certain investment funds are deemed to hold the capital interests and voting rights in the funds managed by them. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For the same purpose, the following instruments qualify as &#147;shares&#148;: (i)&nbsp;shares, (ii)&nbsp;depositary receipts for shares (or negotiable instruments similar to such receipts), (iii)&nbsp;negotiable
instruments for acquiring the instruments under (i)&nbsp;or (ii)&nbsp;(such as convertible bonds), and (iv)&nbsp;options for acquiring the instruments under (i)&nbsp;or (ii). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The AFM requires the notification of any gross short position in our shares. For the notification of gross short positions the same thresholds apply as for notifying an actual or potential interest in the capital
and/or or voting rights of a Dutch listed company, as referred to above. In addition, pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 236/2012, each person holding a net short position amounting to 0.2 percent of the issued share capital of a Dutch listed company is
required to report it to the AFM. Each subsequent increase of this position by 0.1 percent above 0.2 percent will also need to be reported. Each net short position equal to 0.5 percent of the issued share capital of a Dutch listed company and any
subsequent increase of that position by 0.1 percent will be made public via the AFM short selling register. To calculate whether a natural person or legal person has a net short position, their short positions and long positions must be set-off. A
short transaction in a share can only be contracted if a reasonable case can be made that the shares sold can actually be delivered, which requires confirmation of a third party that the shares have been located. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The AFM keeps a public registry of and publishes all notifications made pursuant to the FMSA. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="100%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" ALIGN="center">
